question:
Produce a concise system history of the Tekoa WWTP treatment plant (exclude collection system). Focus on process-side modifications from ~2000–present: headworks (screens/grit), flow equalization, aeration/oxidation ditch/lagoon changes, secondary clarification/filtration, disinfection (UV or chlorination/dechlorination), solids handling (digestion/thickening/dewatering), pumping/blowers/SCADA, and outfall/discharge. Identify project dates, design or permit capacities (e.g., ADF/PF, MGD) and Basis-of-Design/plan/spec references.

Return:
1) A 250–350 word narrative engineering report section with inline citations to primary PDFs (Title, page/figure) only.
2) A 5–10 row summary table with columns: Year/Date | Change/Project | Unit(s) affected | Design capacity impact (MGD) | Citation (Title, p./Fig.).

Prefer primary Engineering Reports, Basis of Design, Plans/Specs, and permits; avoid summaries.

nodes.text:
# 1. Incoming Flow:

Sources: Domestic wastewater from approximately 285 people, plus a small restaurant and convenience store.

# 2. Headworks:

* Components: Grit chamber and self-cleaning screen.  
* Flow Sources:  
  - Scum overflow from the secondary clarifier.  
  - Lift station flow from the collection system.

# 3. Cleanout:

* Function: After passing through the headworks, the flow is directed to a cleanout that includes a pipe from the recycle activated sludge.  
* Discharge: The cleanout discharges to the aeration basin.

# 4. Aeration Basin:

* Design: High-density polyethylene-lined earthen basin.  
* Process: The center portion of the basin receives air to aid in biological treatment.  
* Discharge: Gravity flow of treated wastewater proceeds to the secondary clarifier.

# 5. Secondary Clarifier:

Three Waste Streams:

* Scum Collection: The scum collector gathers treated wastewater and some solids from the surface and recycles the flow to the headworks via a lift pump.  
* Thickened Solids: Solids at the bottom of the clarifier are returned to the aeration basin as return activated sludge (RAS) or sent to the abandoned lagoon as waste activated sludge (WAS). (The aerobic digester, which is currently non-operational, was designed to handle WAS.)  
* Effluent: The clarified effluent enters a quiescent zone around the effluent weir and discharges to UV disinfection.

# 6. UV Disinfection:

* Design: The UV system includes a redundant bulb bank and a U-shaped tank. Flow enters on the north side, passing through both bulb banks, but only one set of bulbs is operated at a time. Bulbs are rotated periodically to ensure even wear.

# 7. Effluent Discharge:

* Effluent Flow: After UV disinfection, the treated effluent flows through an effluent flow gauge.

* 2.4 Chlorine Contact Tank (Modified 1990)
* 2.5 Aerobic Digester (Modifeid 1990)
* 2.6 Polymer Feed System (Installed 1990)
* 2.7 Sludge Drying Beds (Installed 1990)
* 2.8 Flow Monitoring, Chlorination & Dechlorination (Modified 1990)
* 2.9 Yard Hydrants (Modified 1990)

## III. Maintenance
* 3.1 Mechanical Equipment
* 3.2 Collector/Interceptor System

nodes.text:
9. No guidance is given for adjusting the SO2 feed rate to ensure dechlorination and permit compliance. The detection limit for chlorine is above the threshold for toxic effects. It is better for the operator to monitor for SO2 to adjust the dosage for dechlorination.

10. This section should also advise the operator that chlorine is very corrosive in a moist environment. Care should be taken to keep equipment dry and keep caps on to seal out atmospheric moisture.

11. Section 2.9 Yard Hydrants, should this section explain how to purge the vacuum breaker? I would rather see the explanation.

12. Maintenance section, figure 1. There is not much differentiation between the dashed line for sludge and the dashed line for decant. Dots probably would have been a better choice.

13. The engineering report states that the collection system is prone to I/I and even though corrections have been made in the past, shortly thereafter new I/I problems arise. Why then does the O&M report only suggest minimal inspections and action only after significant problems are identified? Preventative inspections and actions will save money and effort.

14. Original design population was 1000, O&M manual table III lists current population of 850 for the design population. Wyatt-Jaykim’s engineering report, section 3.1, of 1988 also uses a design population of 1000.

15. The old O&M manual gives 40,000 gallons as the volume of the aeration basin and 35,000 gallons as the volume of the aerobic digester. The new O&M manual and the design criteria on the plan give 47,000 gallons for volume of aeration basin and 12,000 gallons for volume of digester. I have found no changes identified that explain the volume change in the region’s records. The O&M should have one set of correct volumes and a solids management plan based on the actual volume of solids and recommended MCRT. I agree with 47,000 gal. for aeration basin and 36,500 gal. for digester.

13. The engineering report states that the collection system is prone to I/I and even though corrections have been made in the past, shortly thereafter new I/I problems arise. Why then does the O&M report only suggest minimal inspections and action only after significant problems are identified? Preventative inspections and actions will save money and effort.

14. Original design population was 1000, O&M manual table III lists current population of 850 for the design population. Wyatt-Jaykim’s engineering report, section 3.1, of 1988 also uses a design population of 1000.

15. The old O&M manual gives 40,000 gallons as the volume of the aeration basin and 35,000 gallons as the volume of the aerobic digester. The new O&M manual and the design criteria on the plan give 47,000 gallons for volume of aeration basin and 12,000 gallons for volume of digester. I have found no changes identified that explain the volume change in the region’s records. The O&M should have one set of correct volumes and a solids management plan based on the actual volume of solids and recommended MCRT. I agree with 47,000 gal. for aeration basin and 36,500 gal. for digester.
nodes.text_clean: 9. No guidance is given for adjusting the SO2 feed rate to ensure dechlorination and permit compliance. The detection limit for chlorine is above the threshold for toxic effects. It is better for the operator to monitor for SO2 to adjust the dosage for dechlorination. 10. This section should also advise the operator that chlorine is very corrosive in a moist environment. Care should be taken to keep equipment dry and keep caps on to seal out atmospheric moisture. 11. Section 2.9 Yard Hydrants, should this section explain how to purge the vacuum breaker? I would rather see the explanation. 12. Maintenance section, figure 1. There is not much differentiation between the dashed line for sludge and the dashed line for decant. Dots probably would have been a better choice. 13. The engineering report states that the collection system is prone to I/I and even though corrections have been made in the past, shortly thereafter new I/I problems arise. Why then does the O&M report only suggest minimal inspections and action only after significant problems are identified? Preventative inspections and actions will save money and effort. 14. Original design population was 1000, O&M manual table III lists current population of 850 for the design population. Wyatt-Jaykim’s engineering report, section 3.1, of 1988 also uses a design population of 1000. 15. The old O&M manual gives 40,000 gallons as the volume of the aeration basin and 35,000 gallons as the volume of the aerobic digester. The new O&M manual and the design criteria on the plan give 47,000 gallons for volume of aeration basin and 12,000 gallons for volume of digester. I have found no changes identified that explain the volume change in the region’s records. The O&M should have one set of correct volumes and a solids management plan based on the actual volume of solids and recommended MCRT. I agree with 47,000 gal. for aeration basin and 36,500 gal. for digester.

nodes.text:
# I. REGULATORY REQUIREMENTS FOR FACILITY PLAN/ENGINEERING REPORTS This Wastewater Treatment Plant Facility Plan (Facility Plan) has been prepared for the City of Tekoa, Washington, in accordance with the provisions of Washington Administrative Code (WAC) Chapter 173-240, "Submission of Plans and Reports for Construction of Wastewater Facilities." The WAC requirements for an engineering report (as detailed in WAC 173-240-060) are listed in the table below, cross-referenced to their location within this document. Technical guidance was also drawn from the Washington State Department of Ecology's (Ecology) "Criteria for Sewage Works Design" (Orange Book).
Text from WAC 173-240-060 Inclusion Check Confirmation / Location
060(1) Planning Requirements
The engineering report for a domestic wastewater facility shall include each appropriate (as determined by Ecology) item required in WAC 173-240-050 for general sewer plans unless an up-to-date general sewer plan is on file with Ecology. Normally, an engineering report is not required for sewer line extensions or pump stations. See WAC 173-240-020(13) and 173-240-030(5). The facility plan described in 40 CFR 35 is an “engineering report." The report complies with an up-to-date general sewer plan (WAC 173-240-050) approved by and on file with Ecology, OR the report includes required portions of WAC 173-240-050 as determined through consultation with Ecology if an adequate plan is not on file. No
General sewer plan adequately addresses the current conditions and service area. (If applicable and plan is on file) Yes
060(2) Sufficiently Complete
The engineering report shall be sufficiently complete so that plans and specifications can be developed from it without substantial changes. The report contains sufficient design information to allow an engineer not involved in writing the report to produce construction drawings for the facility without reevaluation of the selected unit processes or more than minor unit sizing modifications. Yes
060(3) Minimum Information Required
(a) The name, address, and telephone number of the owner of the proposed facilities, and their authorized representative. The report includes the name, address, and telephone number of the owner and the owner's authorized representative.

The named authorized representative or position has the authority to sign contracts relating to this project.

A specific project contact person other than the legal representative is identified. 		Section 1.2
(b) A project description including a location map and a map of the present and proposed service area. The project description includes the “where, what, and why” of the report and documents the need for the proposed project.

Planning information established in the approved general sewer plan is referenced, and supplemented or updated as necessary to support

nodes.text:
Text from WAC 173-240-060 Inclusion Check Confirmation / Location
anticipated future permit.
The evaluation considers the potential for regulatory changes that could require more stringent treatment requirements during the operating life of the facility.
The evaluation confirms whether the level of treatment produces a discharge that complies with applicable water quality criteria in Chapter 173-201A WAC.
For municipal WWTPs, an analysis of conventional pollutants (BOD5, TSS, pH, dissolved oxygen, temperature) is included.
For municipal WWTPs, an analysis of pathogens (Enterococci, E. Coli, or Fecal Coliform bacteria) is included.
For municipal WWTPs, an analysis of toxic pollutants commonly present (ammonia, chlorine) is included.
An evaluation of the effects of industrial discharges on final effluent quality and potential for toxic materials to pass through is included. (If applicable)
If the receiving water is on the 303(d) list as impaired, the analysis includes the parameters identified in the impairment listing.
Design values align with waste load allocations established in an approved TMDL. (If applicable)
When Ecology has not established a waste load allocation for an impaired parameter, the analysis demonstrates no reasonable potential to cause or contribute to a water quality standard violation.
The report demonstrates whether the discharge will cause a measurable change in existing water quality at the boundary of any authorized chronic mixing zone (as defined by Ecology's measurable change criteria).
Consultation with regional Ecology staff to determine the level of analysis needed to comply with Antidegradation provisions (WAC 173-201A-300 to 330) has occurred and the analysis is included.
(e) A description of the receiving water, applicable water quality standards, and how water quality standards will be met at the boundary of any applicable dilution zone. (173-201A-10Q WAC) The name, location (river mile, latitude/longitude, waterway segment number, township/range, etc.), and water quality classification of the
